title: Clockwork Current
passage:
Four times a day the water in the Skerrow Race quickens, floods through a two-mile gap between the mainland and a low string of islands, and then falls slack again — and it does all of this on a schedule an engineer can print a year in advance. That last fact is the whole attraction. Wind arrives when it pleases and sunlight quits at dusk, but the tide answers to the moon, and the moon keeps an almanac. For decades that predictability made narrow, fast channels like the Skerrow the standing promise of marine power: here, at last, was a renewable resource you could put on a timetable.

In 2019 a small consortium set out to test the promise. They lowered three squat, slow-turning turbines onto the bed of the Race, wired them to the island grid, and left them to work the current. On one narrow measure the pilot delivered exactly what its backers had described. Through its first three years the array fed power to the islands on a schedule no wind farm could match — you could look at a calendar and say, with fair confidence, when the turbines would be spinning hardest. In its strongest months the array ran close to the output its designers had modelled. "The water," the project's lead engineer, Mariel Toft, likes to say, "showed up when we told everyone it would."

However, the tide was never the difficult part. The difficulty lay in reaching the machines. A turbine bolted to the floor of a channel that runs hard four times a day can be serviced only in the brief, calm minutes of slack water, and only then if the weather at the surface happens to cooperate. Barnacles and weed settled on the blades and had to be scraped away; a seal failed on one unit in the second winter and could not be reached for weeks. Every retrieval meant a specialised vessel, a narrow tidal window, and a crew paid to wait for both. The costs the consortium had once filed quietly under "maintenance" swelled into the line that decided whether the whole project made sense.

Toft is careful not to oversell what her turbines proved. The pilot, she argues, settled a question about the resource and left a harder one open. "We showed the current is bankable — that you can promise a utility a delivery schedule and keep it," she says. "What we did not show is that you can service the thing cheaply. Those are not the same result, and people keep hearing the first when I say the second." A marine economist who reviewed the pilot's accounts put it more bluntly: the tide is free, but the boat is not, and it is the boat that sets the price.

So the tidy slogan — that tidal is the "firm" renewable, the one you can count on — survives only in part. The counting-on is genuine; nobody who watched the Skerrow array doubts that the current will keep its appointments. But a resource you can predict is not yet a resource you can afford, and the distance between the two is measured not in tides but in the price of every trip out to scrape a blade. The islands still draw their power from the Race. Whether the next channel ever gets its turbines will turn less on the water, which behaves, than on the machines, which do not.
— Cordelia Nash

slack water = the short interval, between flood and ebb, when a tidal current briefly stops running

questions.prompt: What is this text mainly about?

options.text: That submerged tidal turbines proved technically unworkable in a channel as fast as the Skerrow Race and had to be shut down for good.
That the Skerrow pilot confirmed the tide's predictability but found the deciding obstacle to be the cost of servicing the turbines, not the resource.
That tidal currents in channels like the Skerrow Race run on a fixed timetable that engineers can forecast a full year in advance.
That tidal power is far more dependable than wind or solar and should therefore replace them along island coasts.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-004 (main idea / whole-text gist), edge position 1. STEM-LAW: 'What is this text mainly about?' names no content and cannot entail the answer. WHY-KEY (B): the arc runs phenomenon (the Race's printable schedule, 'a renewable resource you could put on a timetable') -> evidence (the pilot 'fed power to the islands on a schedule no wind farm could match') -> complication ('However, the tide was never the difficult part. The difficulty lay in reaching the machines ... Every retrieval meant a specialised vessel, a narrow tidal window, and a crew paid to wait for both') -> verdict (paragraph 5: 'a resource you can predict is not yet a resource you can afford'). B paraphrases that two-sided verdict — predictability confirmed, servicing cost decisive — without lifting a distinctive four-word run. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = contradiction / scope_error — the turbines worked ('delivered exactly what its backers had described'; 'The islands still draw their power from the Race'), so 'unworkable ... shut down for good' inverts the outcome; tempting because paragraph 3 dwells on failures (a failed seal, fouled blades). C = detail-as-main / scope_error — the year-ahead predictability is real and stated in paragraph 1, but the essay treats it as the settled, easy half of the story, not its point; tempting because it is a concrete, correct fact. D = surface_word_match / outside_knowledge — recycles the wind-and-solar contrast from paragraph 1 into a 'should replace them' policy claim the text never makes and in fact undercuts by stressing cost; tempting to a reader who reads the predictability praise as an endorsement.

questions.prompt: According to the text, what did the Skerrow array achieve over its first three years?

options.text: It ran at close to its full modelled output in every single month across the whole of the three years.
It supplied the islands with all the electricity they needed and replaced their other sources of power.
It fed power to the islands on a predictable schedule that no wind farm could have matched.
It brought the consortium's maintenance costs well below the figure they had first budgeted for.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-001 (direct detail retrieval), local order. STEM-LAW: the stem names the three-year window but not what was achieved. WHY-KEY (C): paragraph 2 states the array 'fed power to the islands on a schedule no wind farm could match — you could look at a calendar and say, with fair confidence, when the turbines would be spinning hardest.' C paraphrases that predictable-delivery result. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = quantifier_upgrade — the text limits near-modelled output to 'its strongest months', which A inflates to 'every single month across the whole of the three years'; tempting because it echoes the modelled-output line. B = outside_knowledge / quantifier_upgrade — the passage never says the array met all the islands' demand or replaced other sources; it says only that it fed power on schedule; tempting because a working island turbine array sounds self-sufficient. D = wrong_location / contradiction — reverses paragraph 3, where the maintenance costs 'swelled into the line that decided whether the whole project made sense'; tempting because it reuses the passage's own 'maintenance' framing.

questions.prompt: What does the text say made the Skerrow turbines expensive to run?

options.text: They could be serviced only during the brief slack-water window, and only when the surface weather allowed, so each trip out was costly.
They wore out quickly in the fast current and had to be replaced far sooner than the designers had planned.
The tide turned out to be less predictable than forecast, forcing repeated unplanned shutdowns of the array.
Connecting the submerged array to the island grid demanded costly new undersea cabling along the Race.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-001 (direct detail retrieval), local order. STEM-LAW: the stem names that running the turbines was expensive but not why. WHY-KEY (A): paragraph 3 states a turbine on the channel floor 'can be serviced only in the brief, calm minutes of slack water, and only then if the weather at the surface happens to cooperate', and 'Every retrieval meant a specialised vessel, a narrow tidal window, and a crew paid to wait for both.' A paraphrases that access-cost mechanism. WHY-DISTRACTORS: B = outside_knowledge — the passage reports fouled blades and one failed seal but never says the turbines wore out fast or needed early replacement; the cost driver is access for servicing, not attrition; tempting as a plausible guess about machines in a fast current. C = contradiction — the whole premise is that the tide is predictable ('the current will keep its appointments'); attributing the cost to an unpredictable tide inverts the text; tempting because it borrows the predictability theme. D = surface_word_match / outside_knowledge — the array was 'wired ... to the island grid', but the passage never attributes any cost to cabling; the expense it names is retrieval and servicing; tempting because the cabling detail is genuinely present.

questions.prompt: What does Toft's distinction between a 'bankable' current and servicing 'the thing cheaply' imply?

options.text: That the pilot proved the current is bankable, so a utility can be promised a delivery schedule and held to it.
That predictable tidal power is bound to end up cheaper than wind once the turbines have been installed.
That because the current is now bankable, the cost of servicing the turbines is sure to fall over time.
That a tidal resource can be genuinely reliable and predictable while the project built on it stays too expensive to run.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-002 (inference / implication), one logical inch beyond the text. STEM-LAW: the stem quotes the two halves of Toft's distinction but leaves the implication to be drawn. WHY-KEY (D): Toft says 'We showed the current is bankable ... What we did not show is that you can service the thing cheaply. Those are not the same result', and the economist adds 'the tide is free, but the boat is not'. D infers exactly the reliable-yet-unaffordable gap the distinction opens, without overstating it. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = too_literal — a near-restatement of the first half of Toft's quote; it retrieves the bankable claim but performs no inference, and the stem asks what the distinction implies; tempting because it is unarguably true to the text. B = outside_knowledge / quantifier_upgrade — a 'bound to end up cheaper than wind' prediction the passage never makes and its cost emphasis undercuts; tempting to a reader who equates predictable with cheap. C = contradiction — the text gives no ground for servicing costs falling; the economist's 'the boat ... sets the price' points the other way; tempting because 'matures and gets cheaper' is a familiar technology story.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's attitude toward the slogan that tidal is the 'firm' renewable you can count on?

options.text: Convinced: the writer fully endorses tidal as the firm renewable that has now proved it can replace less reliable sources.
Partly persuaded: the writer grants that the current is genuinely dependable but insists dependable is not the same as affordable.
Dismissive: the writer treats the idea that the tide is dependable as a slogan with little real basis behind it.
Detached: the writer sets the engineer's and the economist's views side by side without taking any position of his own.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-005 (stance / tone / attitude), edge position 5; signature trap is scope/attribution swap of the essay's own qualified verdict. STEM-LAW: the stem names the slogan under judgement but not the writer's stance. WHY-KEY (B): paragraph 5 delivers a two-sided verdict — 'the tidy slogan ... survives only in part. The counting-on is genuine ... But a resource you can predict is not yet a resource you can afford.' 'Partly persuaded' names exactly that bounded position. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = scope overshoot — assigns the writer the full endorsement the essay explicitly qualifies ('survives only in part'); tempting because the writer does affirm the predictability warmly. C = polarity overshoot — pushes the reservation into flat dismissal, contradicted by 'The counting-on is genuine; nobody ... doubts that the current will keep its appointments'; tempting to a reader who reads the cost verdict as a total rejection. D = role_or_attribution_swap / tone_misread — 'without taking any position' ignores the writer's own verdict in paragraph 5; the engineer and economist are quoted, but the closing judgement is the writer's; tempting because the middle paragraphs do weigh both voices.

generator_meta.topic: tidal-stream energy pilot at an invented tidal race: the resource proved predictable but the cost of servicing seabed turbines, not the tide, decided the project's economics
fictional_entities: the Skerrow Race and the Skerrow tidal-stream pilot (invented site and project)
the consortium that installed the three turbines in 2019 (invented)
Mariel Toft, lead engineer (invented; all quotes invented)
the unnamed marine economist who reviewed the accounts (invented)
generator_meta.question_geometry: Q1 main idea (edge, pos1) / Q2 detail (three-year achievement) / Q3 detail (why expensive to run) / Q4 inference (bankable != cheap) / Q5 stance (edge, pos5)
generator_meta.key_letters: B, C, A, D, B — all four letters used; no monotonic run, no fixed alternation; keys mix confident-specific (Q2 predictable delivery, Q3 access-cost mechanism) with two-sided/qualified (Q1, Q4, Q5) so neither 'pick the longest' nor 'pick the hedged' scores blind.
generator_meta.engineered_traps: quantifier_upgrade at Q2/A ('strongest months' -> 'every single month'); wrong_location/contradiction at Q2/D (maintenance costs reversed); contradiction at Q3/C (predictable tide inverted) and surface_word_match at Q3/D (cabling detail turned into a cost never attributed); too_literal at Q4/A (first half of Toft's quote restated as a non-inference); outside_knowledge/quantifier_upgrade at Q1/D, Q2/B, Q4/B; scope and polarity swaps of the essay's qualified verdict at Q5/A and Q5/C; attribution swap at Q5/D.
generator_meta.hedge_balance_note: The key is NOT the sole measured option among absolutised distractors. On Q2 and Q3 the key is the confident specific claim while a distractor over-generalises (Q2/A 'every single month', Q2/B 'all the electricity'); on Q1 the specific factual distractor C sits beside the two-sided key B. 'Pick the qualified option' therefore does not track the key across the unit.
generator_meta.law11_note: No 'agreement/most in line with the text' item in this unit; the detail items (Q2, Q3) use wrong_location/contradiction distractors that are identifiably wrong to the stem's target, not verbatim-true details offered beside the key.
generator_meta.self_blind_solve: Solved all five from the passage alone: Q1=B (only option matching the two-sided verdict; A contradicts the working outcome, C is a true sub-point, D is an unstated policy claim), Q2=C (A/B overstate, D reverses), Q3=A (B/C/D each contradicted or unstated), Q4=D (A merely restates, B/C add unstated certainty), Q5=B (A/C overshoot in opposite directions, D denies the writer's own verdict). Each has exactly one defensible answer. All entities fictional; no famous-thesis anchoring.
generator_meta.schema_note: Emits 5 questions per the long_passage_5q blueprint (ELF long passages are invariantly 5q). mech.py accepts 1-5 questions; this intentionally exceeds candidate-item.schema.json questions.maxItems=4, authored for single-question seeded eval items. All other schema constraints pass.
